EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southern Delaware during the late evening and overnight on June 11th. These thunderstorms formed close to a nearly stationary frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was measured at Dewey Beach during a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger thunderstorms during the night of July 1st that produced both heavy downpours and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a townhouse fire on Creekside Court in the Christine Hollow Development in Bear. The fire burned the roof of the townhouse and adjacent townhouses also suffered minor damage. The townhome residents were displaced. Damage was estimated at $125,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong south winds mixed to the ground after a warm front moved through southern Delaware during the evening of the 2nd and early in the morning of the 3rd. Peak wind gust averaged around 55 mph and tore down some weak tree limbs, trees and wires.  ||Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 54 mph at the Brandywine Shoal Light in Delaware Bay.

Very strong south winds mixed to the ground after a warm front moved through southern Delaware during the evening of the 2nd and early in the morning of the 3rd. Peak wind gust averaged around 55 mph and tore down some weak tree limbs, trees and wires.  ||Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 54 mph at the Brandywine Shoal Light in Delaware Bay.

A powerful nor'easter produced rain and strong to high winds across Kent and Sussex Counties from the 11th through the 14th. While rain began falling on the 11th, the heaviest rain fell from the 12th into the first half of the 13th. The rain ended on the 14th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2.5 to 4.5 inches and produced some poor drainage and low lying area flooding. Runoff from streams, creeks and rivers were slowed by the higher than normal tides. This included the Nanticoke River. In Kent County, some roadway flooding was reported in Frederica. The entrance to the Dover Public Library flooded.||Event precipitation totals included 4.62 inches in Greenwood (Sussex County),  4.57 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County), 4.40 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 4.29 inches in Delmar (Sussex County), 3.23 inches in Dover (Kent County), 3.08 inches in Smyrna (Kent County), 2.89 inches in Marydel (Kent County) and 2.12 inches in Lewes (Sussex County).| |The nor'easter was originally Hurricane Ida, which made landfall in Alabama on the morning of the 10th. As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

Freezing rain that started during the morning of the 13th led to several accidents across New Castle County, mainly on bridges and overpasses. Temperatures were just cold enough at the start of the precipitation for it to fall as freezing rain. The Tyler McConnell Bridge northwest of Wilmington was closed due to icing.  About a dozen weather related accidents were reported in New Castle County.   ||Freezing rain began in parts of New Castle County between 7 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST. and changed over to plain rain by 10 a.m. EST. The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.

An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain began falling during the evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th. It fell heavy at times during the early morning of the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged two to three inches across Delaware. The concentration of the heavy rain in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage and field flooding as well as flooding of some of the rivers and creeks within the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 2.86 inches in Felton, 2.49 inches in Smyrna and 2.43 inches at the Dover Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful nor'easter produced wind gusts nearly as high as 60 mph, widespread moderate tidal flooding, heavy rain and severe beach erosion along the Delaware coast from November 12th through the 14th. Preliminary damage was estimated at 45 million dollars. The highest winds occurred from the morning of the 12th into the afternoon of the 13th. About 13,000 people in Delaware and Maryland lost power. The heaviest rain fell on the 11th and 12th. The combination of heavy rain and tides caused about 50 roads in Delaware to be closed, the most notable Delaware State Route 1. Parts of the roadway remain closed through the 15th as three feet of sand accumulated on it. Service on the Cape May Lewes Ferry was limited. The highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 13th. This was the highest tides in Sussex County since the February 5, 1998 nor'easter. Because of  the persistent onshore flow, tidal flooding also occurred in Rehoboth and Indian River Bays. Tidal departures reached four and a half feet. ||Delaware Governor Jack A. Markell declared a state of emergency on the 12th. The National Guard was mobilized to help with possible evacuations. In Sussex County, many schools closed early on the 12th. Voluntary evacuations occurred in Oak Orchard, Riverdale and Mariners Cove. The Community Church in Oak Orchard was used as a shelter by about 100 people. Water rescues were performed on Delaware State Route 1, Long Neck Road and Cannon Street. ||In Lewes, several roads were flooded and closed including Savannah Road. In Rehoboth Beach, half of the dune sand was gone. There was a cut in the dunes north of the Henlopen Hotel. In Dewey Beach, flooding occurred along Rehoboth Bay. In Indian Beach, the dunes broke in several places and waves broke under homes.  Within the Delaware Seashore State Park, there were several dune breaks. One break flooded Delaware State Route 1 with water and sand just north of the Indian River Inlet Bridge. The roadway remained closed through the 15th.  Beach erosion was described as the heaviest in Indian Beach and within the Delaware Seashore State Park. In Oak Orchard, Indian River Bay flooding was ranked as the third worst since the 1962 Ash Wednesday nor'easter. In Bethany Beach, four dunes disappeared and flooding occurred in the north side of the municipality. Waves were measured at 10 feet along the shore. In South Bethany, no dunes were left along the south side of the town.| |Tidal flooding extended upward into Delaware Bay and along tidal sections of the Delaware River, but gradually became minor. The highest tide at Reedy Island in New Castle County was 7.35 feet above mean lower low water on the morning of the 14th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water.||Peak wind gusts were all in Sussex County and included 60 mph in Fenwick Island, 56 mph in Lewes and Rehoboth Beach and  46 mph in Georgetown.  The highest tide at Lewes reached 7.88 feet above mean lower low water  on the morning of the 13th. Moderate tidal flooding ranges from 7.00 to 7.99 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide recorded in Lewes since the February 5, 1998 nor'easter.||The nor'easter was originally Hurricane Ida, which made landfall in Alabama on the morning of the 10th. As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. Of equal importance, was a high pressure system that moved across New England and the Canadian Maritimes from the 12th through the 14th. Its effects were threefold, it increased the difference in surface pressure (thus increased the winds), it blocked the low pressure system from moving offshore and provided a very long fetch of onshore flow. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain produced flooding along sections of the Nanticoke River in western Sussex County. The Nanticoke River near Bridgeville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 407 p.m. EST on the 9th through 1022 a.m. EST on the 10th. It crested at 8.48 feet at 1115 p.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals included 2.28 inches in Greenwood, 2.26 inches in Delmar, 2.16 inches in Seaford, 2.15 inches in Lewes and 2.04 inches in Georgetown.

As the second nor'easter was forming off the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon and early evening on the 17th, gusty northeast winds increased and reached around 50 mph along the immediate coast on lower Delaware Bay and the ocean for a couple of hours in Sussex County. Peak wind gusts included 51 mph in Lewes.

As the second nor'easter was forming off the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon and early evening on the 17th, gusty northeast winds increased and reached around 50 mph along the immediate coast on lower Delaware Bay and the ocean for a couple of hours in Sussex County. Peak wind gusts included 51 mph in Lewes.

Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires across Delaware. The strong winds pushed high profile vehicles out of their lanes in New Castle County. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed, but peak wind gusts were on average a few mph lower than on the 9th.

On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed, but peak wind gusts were on average a few mph lower than on the 9th.

Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening. The combination of the strong northwest winds during the day on the 29th and the larger tide ranges associated with the approaching full moon spring tides produced blowout tides during the afternoon low tide on the 29th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and Upper Delaware Bay. Low tides reached 2.83 feet below mean lower low water at Delaware City (New Castle County) and 2.65 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island (New Castle County). At both locations, low water problems start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Strong northwest winds occurred on the 29th after a cold front moved through Delaware. Winds decreased during the second half of the afternoon after a high pressure system from the Midwest moved closer to the region. Peak wind gusts occurred during the morning, averaged around 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered outages. ||Peak wind gusts included 51 mph at Lewes (Sussex County), 47 mph at the New Castle County Airport and 43 mph in Dover (Kent County).

A major winter storm affected Delaware on the 19th and 20th. Heavy snow fell across New Castle and Kent Counties and a wintry mix of heavy snow, sleet and rain fell across Sussex County. Snowfall averaged 12 to 20 inches with some lower amounts along the Atlantic Coast and southern Sussex County. ||Snow began around Midnight EST on the 19th and fell at its heaviest during the morning in Sussex County and in the afternoon in Kent and New Castle Counties. In Sussex County, the snow changed to sleet and then rain around Noon EST on the 19th before changing back to all snow later that afternoon. The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 20th, stopping in New Castle County last.||The winter storm postponed many planned local and high school sporting events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many municipalities declared states of emergency. Because this occurred over a weekend, the number of traffic accidents was reduced. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were also delayed. The winter storm also caused service interruptions on the 19th and 20th on the Cape May/Lewes Ferry. ||Representative snowfall included 21.2 inches in Greenwood (Sussex County), 18.7 inches in Felton (Kent County), 18.0 inches in Dover (Kent County), 17.0 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 16.8 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County), 16.1 inches in Middletown and Newark (New Castle County) and 6.2 inches in Delmar (Sussex County). ||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

A weak low pressure system dropped a wintry mix of precipitation across New Castle and Kent Counties in Delaware.  Snowfall averaged 1 to 2 inches with ice accretions less than one tenth of an inch. Precipitation began as a wintry mix across Kent County and snow in New Castle County around  4 a.m. EST. The snow changed to a wintry mix before it ended in New Castle County and rain before it ended in Kent County. All precipitation ended by Noon EST.  The precipitation's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents.||Specific snowfall included 1.7 inches at the New Castle County Airport. The low pressure system that caused the wintry mix moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

The first measurable snow of the winter season accumulated 1 to 2 inches across New Castle County. Most of the accumulations occurred on non-paved surfaces, bridges and overpasses. Precipitation started as rain around 6 a.m. EST on the 5th. As the precipitation intensity increased and the low pressure system moved farther offshore, the rain changed to snow around Noon EST. The snow ended by the middle of the evening. Accumulations included 1.5 inches in Newark and 1.0 inch in Bear and at the New Castle County Airport.||The combination of spring tides associated with the recent full moon and the onshore flow around the low pressure system produced minor tidal flooding at the time of the morning high tide in Sussex County. The high tide at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County) reached 6.43 feet above mean lower low water (minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water)||The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that formed off the North Carolina coastal waters during the early morning of the 5th. The low moved rapidly northeast and at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th was already about 200 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey.

An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain began falling during the evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th. It fell heavy at times during the early morning of the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged two to three inches across Delaware. The concentration of the heavy rain in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage and field flooding as well as flooding of some of the rivers and creeks within the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain produced flooding along a couple of creeks and rivers in New Castle County during the morning and afternoon of the 9th along with poor drainage flooding. In Wilmington, a woman and her three children were rescued from flooding on 12th Street. Another water rescue occurred on Governor Printz Boulevard. In Stanton, an injured man was rescued from underpass flooding. Sections of Delaware State Routes 1 and 16 were closed in the county. ||The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 626 a.m. EST through 215 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 11.96 feet at 10 a.m. EST. The White Clay Creek near Newark was above its 13 foot flood stage from 841 a.m. EST through 240 p.m. EST. It crested at 14.15 feet at Noon EST. The Red Clay Creek at Wooddale was above its 7 foot flood stage at 856 a.m. EST through 1212 p.m. EST. It crested at 7.47 feet at 1030 a.m. EST. Farther downstream, the Red Clay Creek at Stanton was above its 15 foot flood stage from 853 a.m. EST through 152 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 15.67 feet at 1130 a.m. EST. ||Event precipitation totals included 2.30 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 2.28 inches in Newport, 2.14 inches in Newark, 2.11 inches in Middletown and 2.08 inches in Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of melting snow and heavy rain produced field, poor drainage and stream and creek flooding in Delaware, especially in New Castle County. Rain began falling during the evening of the 25th and fell at a nearly state rate, approximately half an inch every six hours, until it ended about twenty-four hours later during the evening of the 26th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 2 inches with the highest amounts in New Castle County. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10. 5 foot flood stage from 355 a.m. EST through 750 p.m. EST on the 26th. It crested at 11.41 feet at 130  p.m. EST. The White Clay Creek at Newark was above its 13 foot flood stage from 108 p.m. EST through 552 p.m. EST on the 26th. It crested at 13.32 feet at 315  p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 2.38 inches in Newark, 1.98 inches in Middletown, 1.95 inches in Newport and 1.91 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the heavy snow, the nor'easter produced strong gusty northeast winds along the Delaware coast from the early morning into the early evening of the 19th. This onshore flow also produced minor to moderate tidal flooding during the morning high tide on the 19th in Sussex. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 56 mph In Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph at the Indian River Inlet (Sussex County). Service was interrupted on the Cape May/Lewes Ferry on the 19th and 20th.||The high tide on the morning of the 19th reached 7.15 feet above mean lower low water at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County). Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.00 feet above mean lower low water.||The strong winds and tidal flooding were caused by an intense nor'easter that moved from the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 18th northeast to around Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at sunrise on the 19th and then about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at sunrise on the 20th. The strongest onshore flow occurred on the 19th and then slowly decreased that evening and overnight as the low pressure system moved farther offshore.

Strong to high winds affected Delaware during the day on the 12th. The highest winds occurred shortly after a cold frontal passage around 7 a.m. EST and again around 3 p.m. EST in the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged between 55 and 60 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. The strongest recorded winds were in New Castle County. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 4,600 homes and businesses in Delaware and Cecil County Maryland lost power. A downed tree started a small fire in Wilmington (New Castle County). The high winds caused a tractor-trailer to flip over on southbound Delaware State Route 1 just north of Thompsonville Road in Kent County. The high winds also snapped railroad crossing arms in Clayton (Kent County).||Peak wind gusts included 60 mph at the New Castle County Airport, 55 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County), 50 mph in Bear (New Castle County), 49 mph in Sandtown (Kent County) and 43 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County).||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the late evening hours of the 8th and the early morning hours of the 9th.||The water level at the Reedy Point tidal gage dropped to 1.82 feet below mean lower low water at 2:42 AM EST on the 9th then bottomed out at 2.39 feet below mean lower low water at 4:00 AM EST on the 9th. The water level at the Delaware City tidal gage dropped to 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at 2:48 AM EST on the 9th then bottomed out at 2.50 feet below mean lower low water at 4:12 AM EST on the 9th. Meanwhile, further southeast, the water level at the Lewes tidal gage dropped to 1.88 feet below mean lower low water at 10:48 PM EST on the 8th and bottomed out at 2.31 feet below mean lower low water between 11:36 PM EST and 11:42 PM EST on the 8th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the area generally from south to north during the early morning of the 27th. As milder air then worked in especially above the surface, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain before going over to plain rain during the night of the 27th. The freezing rain and sleet however held on longer across the northern part of the state during the morning of the 28th. The combination of the frozen and freezing precipitation created a high impact event and produced very slippery conditions on the roadways throughout Delaware. State Police reported that even the main roads turned very slippery during the height of the storm, especially once the snow mixed with sleet then changed to freezing rain. The Delaware Department of Transportation (DELDOT) was reported to get a good portion of the roads cleared during the early morning hours of the 28th. Kent County, especially from Dover on northward and also across New Castle County, State Police reported that the roads remained rather icy the longest. Numerous accidents were reported across the state with several injuries. State and County Officials advised people to stay off the roads if at all possible, especially in Kent and New Castle Counties. A power outage was reported in the Claymont area (New Castle County) around 12:15 PM EST on the 28th due to a blown Amtrak transformer. No details were available on what caused the transformer to blow.||Governor Jack Markell declared a two-hour delay on the opening of state government offices on the 28th in New Castle and Kent Counties to allow more time for roads to be cleared. Statewide, the state police reported 123 traffic accidents between 10:00 AM EST on the 27th and 10:00 AM EST on the 28th. The bulk of the crashes occurred in New Castle County where there were 62 reported, 5 of which involved injuries. Administrators closed schools on the 28th in the Smyrna School District and Providence Creek Academy in Clayton (Kent County) because of the wintry weather.||As some milder air moved northward over the snow and ice on the 28th, dense fog developed and contributed to an accident near Felton (Kent County) during the afternoon. A state police officer���s cruiser was struck while at a stop sign at Canterbury and Midstate roads. Dense fog prevented another vehicle from seeing the police cruiser, which rear-ended the police officer���s car. The collision had pushed the police cruiser into the intersection. After the officer got out too check on things, another vehicle smashed into the police cruiser because the driver did not see it due to the poor visibility. The impact destroyed the police cruiser. The driver of the second car was injured.||Some total snowfall reports included; 1.8 inches at both the New Castle County Airport in Wilmington and in Bear (New Castle County) and 1.0 in in Greenwood (Sussex County). Some ice accumulation totals included; 0.25 of an inch at both the New Castle County Airport in Wilmington and in Bear (New Castle County).

A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens and 20s, with minimum temperatures down into the single digits. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values around zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The morning of the 17th was the coldest morning in several years. For example, the New Castle County Airport bottomed out at 5 degrees. This was the coldest morning since January 28, 2005 when the low temperature bottomed out at 1 degree. ||Some specific low temperatures on the morning of the 16th included; 7 degrees at Redden (Sussex County), 10 degrees in Greenwood (Sussex County), 11 degrees at the New Castle County Airport in Wilmington (New Castle County), 12 degrees in Blackbird (New Castle County), 13 degrees at both the Dover Air Force Base (Kent County) and the Georgetown Airport (Sussex County). The wind chills dropped into the single digits during the night of the 15th, however then lowered to between zero and five below zero at most locations on the 16th. These bitterly cold wind chills prevailed through the entire day on the 16th.||Some specific low temperatures on the morning of the 17th included; 0 degrees at Blackbird (New Castle County), 0 degrees at Redden (Sussex County), 3 degrees at Greenwood (Sussex County), 5 degrees at the New Castle County Airport in Wilmington (New Castle County), 5 degrees at the Georgetown Airport (Sussex County), and 6 degrees at the Dover Air Force Base (Kent County). The wind chills dropped to between 2 below zero and 5 above zero during the early evening hours of the 17th. The wind chill values were as low as 5 degrees below zero during the morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature bottomed out at 13 degrees during the early morning of the 16th at the Georgetown airport. At this same location, the temperature bottomed out at 5 degrees during the early morning of the 17th. Some mesonet sites had low temperature readings down to zero, especially on the morning of the 17th. These bitterly cold air temperatures combined with some wind, which produced wind chill values to zero or just below for a time.

A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the late evening hours of the 8th and the early morning hours of the 9th.||The water level at the Reedy Point tidal gage dropped to 1.82 feet below mean lower low water at 2:42 AM EST on the 9th then bottomed out at 2.39 feet below mean lower low water at 4:00 AM EST on the 9th. The water level at the Delaware City tidal gage dropped to 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at 2:48 AM EST on the 9th then bottomed out at 2.50 feet below mean lower low water at 4:12 AM EST on the 9th. Meanwhile, further southeast, the water level at the Lewes tidal gage dropped to 1.88 feet below mean lower low water at 10:48 PM EST on the 8th and bottomed out at 2.31 feet below mean lower low water between 11:36 PM EST and 11:42 PM EST on the 8th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds affected Delaware from the afternoon of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. The strong winds began after a cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 22nd. Most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the evening of the 22nd into the early morning of the 23rd. Winds did not diminish until the evening of the 23rd. | |Peak wind gusts included 46 mph in Sandtown (Kent County), 45 mph at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County) and 44 mph at the New Castle County Airport. ||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system as it moved from the Great Lakes and through the Canadian Maritimes and a strong high pressure system that was building east from the Upper Mississippi Valley. ||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in Delaware. It was the driest February on record at the New Castle County Airport (0.30 inches). On a statewide average, it was also the driest February on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation of 0.30 inches was only about 10 percent of the normal February precipitation.

An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the southern portions of the state, then gradually moved northward on the 28th. The local visibilities lowered to under one quarter of a mile at times during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||Dense fog on the 28th contributed to an accident near Felton (Kent County) during the afternoon. A State Police officer���s cruiser was struck while at a stop sign at Canterbury and Midstate roads. Dense fog prevented another vehicle from seeing the police cruiser, which rear-ended the police officer���s car. The collision had pushed the police cruiser into the intersection. After the officer got out too check on things, another vehicle smashed into the police cruiser because the driver did not see it due to the poor visibility. The impact destroyed the police cruiser. The driver of the second car was injured.

Strong to high winds affected Delaware during the day on the 12th. The highest winds occurred shortly after a cold frontal passage around 7 a.m. EST and again around 3 p.m. EST in the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged between 55 and 60 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. The strongest recorded winds were in New Castle County. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 4,600 homes and businesses in Delaware and Cecil County Maryland lost power. A downed tree started a small fire in Wilmington (New Castle County). The high winds caused a tractor-trailer to flip over on southbound Delaware State Route 1 just north of Thompsonville Road in Kent County. The high winds also snapped railroad crossing arms in Clayton (Kent County).||Peak wind gusts included 60 mph at the New Castle County Airport, 55 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County), 50 mph in Bear (New Castle County), 49 mph in Sandtown (Kent County) and 43 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County).||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

While the snowfall was light, less than an inch, the combination of the light coating of snow, temperatures below freezing and the development of fog lead to icy conditions.||The Delaware State Police reported several icy roads in Sussex County during the morning of the 19th. There were several accidents reported between 6:00 AM EST and 12:30 PM EST, some with personal injuries and also property damage. The conditions improved during the early afternoon hours on the 19th as temperatures climbed above freezing.

A winter storm occurred across Delaware from the late afternoon of the 1st into the late afternoon of the 2nd. About five to ten inches of snow fell across the region. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow made travel difficult at best and made it tough for road crews to keep thoroughfares opened. There were many fender benders and vehicles that slid off roads. More than 300 accidents were reported statewide. The Mid-Atlantic AAA responded to 2,600 calls for assistance throughout their region. All schools as well as many state, county and municipal offices were closed on Monday March 2nd. Many businesses and colleges were also closed. Many daytime and evening meetings were postponed. Schools did not reopen until the 4th in New Castle and Kent Counties and the 5th in Sussex County. ||Precipitation began as a mixture of rain and sleet across the lower part of the state and just plain snow in the northern part of the state during the late afternoon and early evening of the 1st. The mixed precipitation changed to all snow by the start of the evening of the 1st and fell heavy at times during the evening and overnight as gravity wave bands of heavier snow moved through the region. Snow of generally lighter intensity fell during the day on the 2nd, before it ended during the late afternoon.||In New Castle County, there were 80 reported snow related accidents, six of them caused injuries. In Kent County, there were 122 snow related accidents. Fifty-six of those resulted in vehicular damage and two had injuries. There was also 64 reported disabled vehicles. In Sussex County, road crews had a difficult time keeping roadways open as considerable drifting occurred, especially in the western part of the county. There were 183 reported snow related accidents, 106 of which resulted in vehicular damage and four injuries. There were also 73 disabled vehicles. Mailboxes were also not immune as 58 of them were damaged during plowing operations.  ||Snowfall totals included 9.7 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 9.0 inches in Houston (Kent County), 8.8 inches in Viola (Kent County), 8.5 inches in Frankford (Sussex County), 6.2 inches in Blackbird (New Castle County), 6.0 inches in Lewes and Bethany Beach (both Sussex County), 5.1 inches in Dover (Kent County), 5.0 inches in Rehoboth (Sussex County) and 4.2 inches in Bridgeville (Sussex County).| |The winter storm was caused by a nor'easter that moved east of the region. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. It was passing through the Canadian Maritimes during the morning of the 3rd. Heavy snow bands associated with the gravity wave occurred during the pre-dawn hours on the 2nd when most of the heaviest snow fell.
A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March afternoon helped trigger severe thunderstorms in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees on Talley Road in Brandywine Hundred north of Interstate 95 and east of United States Route 202.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high winds occurred just after Midnight EST on March 2nd in Kent and Sussex Counties as a gravity wave helped mix stronger winds aloft to the ground. Peak wind gusts reached around 60 mph in Sussex County. The combination of the wind and heavy snow helped cause power outages across the Delmarva Peninsula. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 26,000 homes and businesses lost power in its service area and Delaware Electric Cooperative reported about 14,400 homes and businesses lost power in the state. All power was restored by 8 a.m. EST on the 3rd. The strong to high winds also caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and made it difficult for crews to keep roads plowed and open. The high winds and waves caused beach erosion along the Atlantic coast as well as dune damage in Bethany Beach. Waves even reached the edge of the dunes in Delaware Bay in Lewes. Peak wind gusts included 63 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 62 mph at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County), 53 mph at Indian River (Sussex County), 51 mph at Slaughter Beach (Sussex County) and 45 mph in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County). The strong to high winds were associated with the nor'easter that brought the heavy snow to the state. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning of the 2nd as a gravity wave that emanated from the low pressure system passed across the state.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail fell in northeastern New Castle County in Wilmington and Brandywine Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the Eastern Seaboard and an upper air disturbance that moved across from the Great Lakes, produced two periods of light snow across Delaware. Snowfall from both events totaled around an inch.  ||Precipitation started as light rain between 2 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Precipitation changed to light snow by around 7 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Light snow fell during the day with most of it melting on contact with the ground. The snow ended during the evening of the 3rd. Then bands of light snow showers moved across the state during the first half of the day on the 4th. Some light accumulations occurred before sunrise on the 4th. ||The overnight snowfall led to six motor vehicle accidents in Kent County, mainly on snow covered secondary roads. Two injuries were reported. Snowfall totals included 1.2 inches at the New Castle County Airport and 1.0 inch in Dover (Kent County).||The low pressure system that contributed to the snow formed on a stalled cold front over the southeastern United States on the 2nd. By 7 a.m. EST on the 3rd, it was located about 200 miles east of the Delmarva Peninsula and at 7 p.m. EST that evening it was approaching Nova Scotia. An upper air disturbance then helped drag snow showers southeast from the Great Lakes.

Gusty west to northwest winds affected Delaware from the afternoon of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. The strong winds began after a cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 22nd. Most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the evening of the 22nd into the early morning of the 23rd. Winds did not diminish until the evening of the 23rd. | |Peak wind gusts included 46 mph in Sandtown (Kent County), 45 mph at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County) and 44 mph at the New Castle County Airport. ||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system as it moved from the Great Lakes and through the Canadian Maritimes and a strong high pressure system that was building east from the Upper Mississippi Valley. ||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in Delaware. It was the driest February on record at the New Castle County Airport (0.30 inches). On a statewide average, it was also the driest February on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation of 0.30 inches was only about 10 percent of the normal February precipitation.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the central and northern Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 19th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 49 mph at the New Castle County Airport. The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system approaching from the Mississippi Valley.

A weak onshore flow from the Atlantic Ocean that preceded the passage of a warm front helped produce dense fog across northern Delaware during the first half of the day on the 16th. The dense fog dissipated during the morning of the 16th as a warm front moved north of the state.
Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in Delaware on the morning of the 6th as a warm front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped a pole on Delaware State Route 9 in East Dover Hundred south of Little Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in Delaware on the morning of the 6th as a warm front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes caused several homes in Little Creek to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper air disturbance created an unstable atmosphere and permitted isolated thunderstorms to form around the greater Philadelphia metropolitan area during the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AirTran jet that was heading for landing at Philadelphia International Airport was struck by lightning. It landed without incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow preceding a cold front produced wind gusts to around 45 mph during the afternoon of the 3rd in Sussex County. The peak wind gust in Georgetown was 46 mph.
A strong southerly flow preceding a cold front produced wind gusts to around 45 mph during the afternoon of the 3rd in Sussex County. The peak wind gust in Georgetown was 46 mph.
An approaching cold front helped trigger thunderstorms during the evening of the 21st in Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire damaged a first floor back room of a house on Gates Circle in Hockessin (Mill Creek Hundred). The fire was under control at 930 p.m. EDT that evening and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving and intense low pressure system was responsible for strong northwest winds across Delaware from the evening of the 3rd through the afternoon of the 4th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph. Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Sandtown (Kent County), 46 mph at the Dover Air Force Base (Kent County) and New Castle County Airport and 42 mph at Jone's Crossroad (Sussex County). ||The strong northwest winds began after a cold front moved through the state during the evening on the 3rd. It persisted through the afternoon of the 4th until the low pressure system associated with that front moved farther northeast up the Saint Lawrence River Valley and started to weaken.

Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in Delaware on the morning of the 6th as a warm front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and caused minor damage to the bell tower of the Zion Methodist Church on County Route 382 in Baltimore Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore northeast winds occurred during the afternoon of April 15th as a nor'easter moved east off the Delmarva Peninsula in southern Delaware. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 53 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Sandtown (Kent County). ||The strong northeast winds were caused by the pressure difference between a high pressure system moving across southern Canada and a low pressure system that moved east off the Delmarva Peninsula during the early morning of the 15th.

Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 53 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Sandtown (Kent County). ||The strong northeast winds were caused by the pressure difference between a high pressure system moving across southern Canada and a low pressure system that moved east off the Delmarva Peninsula during the early morning of the 15th.

Strong onshore northeast winds occurred during the afternoon of April 15th as a nor'easter moved east off the Delmarva Peninsula in southern Delaware. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 53 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Sandtown (Kent County). ||The strong northeast winds were caused by the pressure difference between a high pressure system moving across southern Canada and a low pressure system that moved east off the Delmarva Peninsula during the early morning of the 15th.

Strong south winds occurred during the day on the 14th mainly across the southern half of Delaware. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 48 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County) and 40 mph in Sandtown (Kent County). The strong south winds were caused by the pressure difference between a high pressure system in the nearby Atlantic Ocean and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley.

Strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved across Delaware during the afternoon of the 29th. The thunderstorms preceded a cold front that moved through the Middle Atlantic States during the night of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter reported quarter size hail in Houston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved across Delaware during the afternoon of the 29th. The thunderstorms preceded a cold front that moved through the Middle Atlantic States during the night of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Milford. In addition, some small tree branches were torn down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms preceding a cold front moved through Delaware during the afternoon of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in and around Middletown. About 120 Delmarva Power and Light customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred during the day on the 14th mainly across the southern half of Delaware. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 48 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County) and 40 mph in Sandtown (Kent County). The strong south winds were caused by the pressure difference between a high pressure system in the nearby Atlantic Ocean and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley.
A slow southward moving cold front located over the Philadelphia Delaware Valley during the morning of June 3rd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in northern Delaware during that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires and caused power outages in Roxana (Baltimore Hundred).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved across Delaware during the afternoon of the 29th. The thunderstorms preceded a cold front that moved through the Middle Atlantic States during the night of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire sparked a garage and house fire on Oyster Shell Cove in Salt Pond in Baltimore Hundred. The garage was struck by lightning and the fire then spread to the home. Damage was estimated at $40,000. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Leatherstocking Region of New York State during the morning of June 2nd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in northern Delaware during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same severe thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Leatherstocking Region of New York State during the morning of June 2nd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in northern Delaware during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 76 mph at the New Castle County Airport. The same severe thunderstorm caused a partial roof collapse at an industrial park near New Castle County Airport, knocked down large trees in New Castle, knocked down a tree onto a vehicle in Wilmington and capsized seven boats at Battery Park on the Delaware River. The worst reported wind damage was the partial collapse of the roof of an auto parts storage facility near the airport. About a 200 foot section of the roof was torn away, another piece collapsed into the building. Pieces of the roof landed in the back yards of homes on Bunker Hill Road. Two front doors were also blown away. Two homes in New Castle suffered roof damage after trees fell on them. There were about thirty weather related incidences (downed trees and wires and transformers) reported throughout New Castle County. No injuries were reported. About 15,000 homes and businesses lost power because of the severe thunderstorm. Power was fully restored by the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary and moved across Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail also fell east of Hartley in Kenton Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary and moved across Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage between Smyrna and Blackiston Crossroads mainly in Kenton Hundred. There were nine calls for fallen trees and downed wires. In Smyrna shingles were torn from one home and a downed tree damaged a porch. Between Clayton and Blackiston, two mobile homes were damaged after trees fell on them. Another mobile home was moved off its foundation. A barn was destroyed by the thunderstorm winds and a camper within it was damaged.  One hundred pound propane tanks were toppled. Several homes in the Plantation Crossing development west of Clayton suffered minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary and moved across Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail fell in Clayton. Hail covered the ground so extensively, that some of it was still around the next morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern Delaware during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of straight-line wind damage up to one-half mile wide in spots from just west of Harrington in southwestern Kent County in Mispillion Hundred south to the town of Greenwood in northern Sussex County.  Damage began on High Stump Road west of Harrington where a few tree tops were snapped and a few other trees were uprooted. An old barn was blown down, with its roofing material blown up to one-half mile away.  Farther south, two trees were blown down along Burrsville Road. Wind damage continued into Sussex County. ||Corn was stripped to the stalk on many fields within the damage swath, resulting in a total loss in some cases.  Wheat and barely fields also suffered substantial damage.  Combined Kent and Sussex County crop losses could easily total several hundred thousand dollars.  Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern Delaware during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of straight-line wind damage up to one-half mile wide in spots from southwestern Kent County into western Sussex County.  Most of the wind damage occurred in Northwest Fork Hundred. Maximum wind gusts were estimated at 80 mph and caused property as well as substantial crop damage.  One home sustained substantial damage.  There was widespread crop damage from large hail.||Wind damage in Sussex County started near County Route 16 near Blanchard. The most significant damage occurred parallel to McDowell Road east of Woodbridge Road.  A porch roof was ripped away from a wood-framed farm house, and a steel antenna tower next to the house was bent.  A tree near the house was blown down, and a large chicken barn near the house collapsed.  Farther east, another steel antenna tower was bent, and some shingles were torn off the roof of a nearby house. ||Corn was stripped to the stalk on many fields within the damage swath, resulting in a total loss in some cases.  Wheat and barely fields also suffered substantial damage.  Crop losses mainly from hail could easily total several hundred thousand dollars.  ||Tree damage was also noted in the town of Greenwood.  Small to medium sized limbs were torn away along Minor Street in downtown Greenwood.  Some lawn furniture was blown around, and streets and driveways were littered with leaves.  Some funnel clouds were reported, but they did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong thunderstorms moved through Delaware during the morning of June 9th. Lightning from the thunderstorms caused a couple of house fires in Rehoboth Beach and Dagsboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rehoboth Beach, a lightning strike and ensuing fire caused about an estimated $250,000 in damage to a house on Coventry Lane in the Rehoboth Beach Yacht and Country Club development. Lightning apparently struck the attic of the home. Six fire companies fought the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong thunderstorms moved through Delaware during the morning of June 9th. Lightning from the thunderstorms caused a couple of house fires in Rehoboth Beach and Dagsboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Dagsboro, an attic fire caused by a lightning strike to a home on Westchester Court caused an estimated $1,500 in damage to it before it was extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of weak onshore flow and some of the highest spring tides of the year because of lunar perigee coinciding with the new moon caused minor to moderate tidal flooding during the late afternoon and evening high tide cycle from June 21st through June 23rd along the ocean as well as into Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The highest tides occurred during the evening and night of the 22nd when some moderate tidal flooding was reported.| |The highest tides included 6.77 feet above mean lower low water in Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County), 7.69 feet above mean lower low water at the Brandywine Shoal Light in Delaware Bay and 7.75 feet at Reedy Island (New Castle County).  With the exception of Reedy Island, minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.70 feet above mean lower low water. At Reedy Island, minor tidal flooding starts at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.

Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Delaware during the evening of the 25th near a low pressure system that formed along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires across roadways west of Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm in northern Delaware during the evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in Hockessin (Mill Creek Hundred).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm in northern Delaware during the evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in northwestern New Castle County and hit the Hockessin area of Mill Creek Hundred the hardest. Millcreek Road and Evanson Road were closed because of downed trees. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county, about 125 were still without power the night of the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms across southern Delaware during the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm ripped the siding from a building on Grant Avenue in Fenwick Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening of the 20th. The state barely made it into the warm sector (south of the warm front and east of the approaching cold front) of a low pressure system that moved through Pennsylvania and New Jersey on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down power lines and large tree limbs in Stanton in Mill Creek Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern Delaware during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also produced large hail west of Greenwood in Sussex County.  There was widespread crop damage from large hail. The hail caused corn to be stripped to the stalk. Wheat and barely fields also suffered substantial damage.  Crop losses could easily total several hundred thousand dollars.  In the nearby garden, strawberry plants were pulverized by the hail. The most significant hail damage occurred parallel to McDowell Road east of Woodbridge Road. Hail size was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern Delaware during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted during a severe thunderstorm in northwest Sussex County. A National Weather Service damage survey indicated that all of the wind damage was straight line and that the funnel did not touch the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern Delaware during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Broad Creek Hundred northeast of Laurel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening of the 20th. The state barely made it into the warm sector (south of the warm front and east of the approaching cold front) of a low pressure system that moved through Pennsylvania and New Jersey on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore a couple of trees in Millville (Baltimore Hundred).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred in southern Delaware during the early evening of the 22nd as upper air disturbances circulated around an upper level low pressure system east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter measured a wind gust to 60 mph during a severe thunderstorm in Dagsboro Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across the area around Noon EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees and wires in Newark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across the area around Noon EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blew a trailer onto U.S. Route 13 in Bridgeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across the area around Noon EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires across the northern tier of Delaware. About 16,000 homes and businesses lost power in New Castle County. Half of them were still without power at 9 p.m. EDT that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points in the mid 70s) was across the area. This resulted in high heat indices in the region, with peak values of 105 degrees at Georgetown and Dover. Temperatures were the highest of the year with highs in the mid 90s. A cold front moved through that night and ended the brief spell of hot weather. High temperatures included 96 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County), 94 degrees un Smyrna (Kent County) and 93 degrees at the New Castle County Airport.

Hurricane Bill passed well east of the Mid Atlantic coast during the 22nd, while a cold front was approaching from the west. Swells from Hurricane Bill led to rough surf, erosion and numerous and strong rip currents from Friday the 21st through Monday the 24th. The worst conditions occurred on Saturday the 22nd as Bill made his closest pass to the region. Waves reached up to 10 feet along the coast and led to numerous water rescues. Two men  were injured after they were pounded into the surf at Rehoboth Beach and North Shores. The latter person was injured after he rescued a boy in the rough surf. No minor tidal flooding was reported along the coast due to increased seas.

A strong cold front was approaching from the west during the 22nd, while Hurricane Bill passed well to our east. An abundance of moisture in place combined with enough instability, which resulted in showers and thunderstorms with locally torrential rainfall and flooding. Two day event precipitation totals ranged from around 1.5 to around 6.5 inches, highest in Kent County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused considerable roadway and field flooding as well as flooding of smaller streams in central and western Kent County. In Harrington, senior citizens were evacuated from the Heritage Manor Apartment Complex after water became knee deep on nearby streets. Event precipitation totals included 6.52 inches in Hartley, 6.50 inches six miles west of Dover. 3.60 inches within Dover and 3.20 inches in Harrington.

A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th. Delmarva Power and Electric reported about 17,500 of its customers lost power across the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms produced wind damage across New Castle County, with the hardest hit areas in the northern part of the county. Numerous trees were uprooted and damaged, especially Tulip Poplars. In Newark, a couple of trees fell on homes and the siding was ripped away from one apartment building. In and around Wilmington, a large tree crashed through a home in Westover Hills. The Hagley Museum and Library lost eight trees. Elsmere and Hockessin were among the communties that were hardest hit by power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th. Delmarva Power and Electric reported about 17,500 of its customers lost power across the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in the northeast part of Sussex County. About 400 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th. Delmarva Power and Electric reported about 17,500 of its customers lost power across the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several large tree limbs and wires across the northern half of Kent County. A measured wind gust of 52 mph was recorded at the Dover Air Force Base.

As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th. Delmarva Power and Electric reported about 17,500 of its customers lost power across the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of large tree limbs and wires in Harrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms across southern Delaware during the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Fenwick Island. The hail was starting to accumulate on the ground before it stopped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west produced strong to isolated severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the late afternoon of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused roof damage to a building within the Enterprise Building Park in Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the state Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st. Most of the wind damage occurred as a line of severe thunderstorms known as a bow echo moved across the state. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 8,000 homes and businesses lost power across the Delmarva Peninsula. Power was fully restored by August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted over Newark, but it did not touch down and cause any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the state Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st. Most of the wind damage occurred as a line of severe thunderstorms known as a bow echo moved across the state. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 8,000 homes and businesses lost power across the Delmarva Peninsula. Power was fully restored by August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the funnel cloud did not touch down, the parent severe thunderstorm did produce downburst straight line winds that tore down tree limbs and wires in Newark.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms tore down trees and wires in western Kent County in Hazlettville and Sandtown. A downed tree near Smyrna closed both southbound lanes of U.S. Route 13.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Frankford.

Delmarva Power and Light reported about 8,000 homes and businesses lost power across the Delmarva Peninsula. Power was fully restored by August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large tree limbs onto power lines on Fox Road in Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon and early evening across New Castle County on the 24th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.0 to 2.0 inches with the heaviest rain occurring across the northeast part of the county. This caused poor drainage flooding and flooding along sections of the Christina River later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain earlier in the evening on the 24th caused minor flooding along sections of the Christina River. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 945 p.m. EDT through 1046 p.m. EDT on the 24th. It crested at 10.59 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.82 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 1.80 inches in Newark and 1.26 inches in Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds preceded the arrival of a cold front across Sussex County Delaware during the afternoon of the 24th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Georgetown and 43 mph in Lewes.

Strong southwest winds preceded the arrival of a cold front across Sussex County Delaware during the afternoon of the 24th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph in Georgetown and 43 mph in Lewes.

A pair of low pressure systems that emerged from the Gulf Of Mexico and passed southeast of Delaware on the 27th and 28th gave the state nearly 48 hours of steady rain from the evening of the 26th into the afternoon of the 28th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 5 inches with the highest amounts in Kent and Sussex Counties. The heaviest rain fell during the early morning of the 27th and again during the early morning of the 28th as the low pressure systems made their closest passes to Delaware. The heavy rain caused stream flooding, field flooding as well as some poor drainage flooding in the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from two days of steady rain caused flooding along sections of the Christina River. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 543 a.m. EDT through 915 a.m EDT on the 28th.  It crested at 10.98 feet at 715 a.m. EDT. Two day event precipitation totals included 2.01 inches in Middletown, 1.88 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 1.81 inches in Bear and 1.70 inches in Newark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of low pressure systems that emerged from the Gulf Of Mexico and passed southeast of Delaware on the 27th and 28th gave the state nearly 48 hours of steady rain from the evening of the 26th into the afternoon of the 28th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 5 inches with the highest amounts in Kent and Sussex Counties. The heaviest rain fell during the early morning of the 27th and again during the early morning of the 28th as the low pressure systems made their closest passes to Delaware. The heavy rain caused stream flooding, field flooding as well as some poor drainage flooding in the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from two days of heavy rain caused poor drainage and field flooding in eastern Sussex County.  In Milton, flooding was reported at the Shipbuilders Village on Spinmaker Lane. Ponding of water occurred at the Cannery Village near the Sussex County Airport in Georgetown and Plantation Road south of Belltown was also nearly impassable.  Event precipitation totals included 4.73 inches in Georgetown, 4.16 inches in Greenwood, 3.96 inches in Seaford and 2.53 inches in Delmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across Delaware from the middle of the morning into the early evening on the 7th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down some weak tree limbs. The speed limit on the Delaware Memorial Bridge was reduced from 50 mph to 25 mph. Measured wind gusts included 52 mph at the New Castle County Airport, 49 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 44 mph in Dover (Kent County) and 43 mph in Frankford (Sussex County).  The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area.  As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.

A pair of nor'easters caused minor to moderate tidal flooding along the ocean and  Delaware Bay from the evening high tide of the 15th into the morning high tide of the 19th. They also caused minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River from the morning high tide of the 16th through the morning high tide of the 17th. Tidal flooding extended into the inlets. In addition to the tidal flooding, heavy surf contributed to and exacerbated the erosion along the ocean and lower Delaware Bay. A few roadways were flooded and closed.||In Sussex County, sections of Delaware State Route 1 were flooded and closed from the 16th through the 18th from Dewey Beach south into Fenwick Island. The Indian River Inlet Bridge was also closed. A couple of roads were flooded and closed in Lewes and Dewey Beach. In Lewes, flooding along the Lewes and Rehoboth Canal threatened, but did not reach any homes. In Indian River Bay, River Road in Oak Orchard was flooded. In Dewey Beach, Crabbers Cove flooded the morning of the 17th.   ||Along the Sussex County beaches, scarping and erosion occurred. Sand fencing and dunes were damaged in Bethany Beach, South Bethany and Dewey Beach. In South Bethany, four dune crossings were closed. Waves at Bethany Beach reached as high as 9 feet.||Along the ocean and Delaware Bay, minor tidal flooding began with the afternoon high tide on the 15th. Moderate tidal flooding then occurred with the high tides on the mornings of the 16th and 18th and both high tides on the 17th. The highest tides occurred during the morning high tide on the 17th. At Breakwater Harbor (Lewes - Sussex County), the tide reached 7.37 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water.  Along tidal sections of the Delaware River in New Castle County, minor tidal flooding occurred from the morning of the 16th into the morning of the 17th. The highest tides occurred during the morning high tide on the 16th. At Reedy Island, the tide reached 7.42 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The first nor'easter moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 14th to the northern North Carolina coastal waters on the morning of the 15th and then moved rapidly northeast and by sunrise on the 16th was already southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The second nor'easter formed off the Delmarva coastal waters on the 17th and drifted very slowly east farther offshore as it intensified slowly. It was located about 250 miles east of Cape May, New Jersey during the afternoon of the 18th and then accelerated to the northeast and passed through Nova Scotia on the morning of the 19th. As these nor'easters formed and moved offshore, a high pressure system remained over nearby Canada and northern New England; both combined to maintain the onshore flow.
